title: Beyond Determinism: Notes on Statistics
summary: Lesson 5 (parts 1 & 2). Statistics is the **science of data**: from a finite **sample** we infer the properties of the underlying **population**. You will learn the role of **estimators** (sample mean and sample variance, and *why* we divide by $n-1$), the **Normal / Gaussian** distribution and its **standardisation** $Z=(X-\mu)/\sigma$, the **Central Limit Theorem** with the **standard error** $\sigma/\sqrt n$, **confidence intervals**, and the three sampling distributions the exam needs — **Student t**, **Pearson $\chi^2$** and **Fisher F** — each illustrated with an interactive explorer.
objectives: Distinguish population (with parameters $\mu, \sigma^2$) from sample (size $n$), and define a statistic/estimator $T=t(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ and its estimate.

Use the sample mean $\bar X_n$ as a correct & efficient estimator of $\mu$, and the sample variance $s_n^2=\tfrac{1}{n-1}\sum (X_i-\bar X_n)^2$ as the correct estimator of $\sigma^2$, and explain why the denominator is $n-1$.
Describe the Normal distribution $X\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)$, standardise with $Z=(X-\mu)/\sigma$, and read the 68/95/99.7 and $\pm1.96\sigma$ (95%) areas.
State the Central Limit Theorem and its consequence $\bar X_n\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2/n)$, with standard error $\sigma/\sqrt n$ ($n\ge30$ for a good approximation).
Build a confidence interval for $\mu$ with known variance ($z$) and unknown variance (Student $t$), and interpret it correctly.
Identify what each sampling distribution is for: Student $t$ (mean with unknown $\sigma$), $\chi^2$ (variance of a normal population), Fisher $F$ (ratio of two variances).

blocks.text: 1. Population, sample, and statistical inference

**Statistics** is the branch of mathematics that studies methods of **collecting, analysing and reporting data** to make decisions and solve problems — it is the **science of data**. We rarely have all the data of the whole **population**, so we take a finite **sample** $\{x_1,\dots,x_n\}$ (sample size $n$) and **infer** the population's properties.

- **Sampling**: drawing a sample from the population.
- **Inference**: estimating the population parameters from the sample.

A **statistic** $g(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ is a function of the (i.i.d.) random variables of the sample that **does not contain any unknown parameter** — so it is itself a random variable. An **estimator** $T=t(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ is a statistic used to estimate a parameter $\theta$ (e.g. $\mu$); a particular value $\hat\theta=t(x_1,\dots,x_n)$ is an **estimate**. Note: $\bar X_n-\mu$ is **not** a statistic, because it contains the unknown $\mu$.

blocks.title: What makes an estimator good?
blocks.text:
- **Correct (unbiased)**: $\mathbb{E}[T]=\theta$.
- **Consistent**: $\mathrm{Var}[T]\to 0$ as $n\to\infty$.
- **Efficient**: among correct estimators, the one with the **smallest variance** is the most efficient.
2. The estimators: sample mean and sample variance

blocks.title: Sample mean (estimator of μ)
blocks.caption: The sample mean is a **correct and efficient** estimator of the population expected value $\mu$: it is unbiased ($\mathbb{E}[\bar X_n]=\mu$) and consistent (its variance $\sigma^2/n\to0$).

blocks.title: Sample variance (estimator of σ²)
blocks.caption: Dividing by **$n-1$** (not $n$) makes $s_n^2$ a **correct (unbiased)** estimator of the population variance $\sigma^2$ (Excel **VAR.S**, not VAR.P).

blocks.title: Why $n-1$? (degrees of freedom)
blocks.text: The deviations are taken from the **sample mean** $\bar X_n$, which is itself computed from the same data. The $n$ deviations $X_i-\bar X_n$ must sum to zero, so only **$n-1$ of them are free** — one degree of freedom is 'used up' estimating the mean. Dividing by $n$ would systematically **underestimate** $\sigma^2$; dividing by $n-1$ corrects the bias exactly. (Note: $s_n$ is not a perfectly unbiased estimator of $\sigma$, but the bias is negligible for $n>30$.)
3. The Normal (Gaussian) distribution and standardisation

In experimental applications the **Normal distribution** is crucial: it describes the **normal effect of chance**. When systematic errors and measurement accidents have been avoided, the main effect is due to many small random errors — and (Central Limit Theorem) data influenced by many small, independent random effects are approximately **normally distributed**. The bell shape emerges even from summing a few uniform variables (e.g. the sum of three dice).

blocks.title: Normal PDF
blocks.caption: $\mu$ (mean) sets the **central position**; $\sigma$ (standard deviation) sets the **width**. For the Normal, $x=\mu$ is simultaneously the **mode**, the **median** and the mean, and the curve has inflection points at $x=\mu\pm\sigma$.

blocks.title: Normal distribution explorer
blocks.caption: Drag $\mu$ to slide the bell along the axis and $\sigma$ to widen/narrow it. Watch how the area under a fixed interval changes — and confirm the standard ratios on the next slide.

blocks.title: Standardisation
blocks.caption: The standard normal $Z$ has mean 0 and standard deviation 1. Its CDF $F(z)$ has **no closed form**, so it is read from tables: for negative $z$, $F(-z)=1-F(z)$. A probability for $X$ becomes $\mathbb{P}(a<X<b)=F(z_b)-F(z_a)$ with $z=(x-\mu)/\sigma$.

blocks.title: Memorise the standard areas
blocks.text: Symmetric intervals around $\mu$ contain: $\mu\pm\sigma\to68.3\%$, $\mu\pm1.645\sigma\to90\%$, $\mu\pm1.96\sigma\to95\%$, $\mu\pm2\sigma\to95.45\%$, $\mu\pm2.576\sigma\to99\%$, $\mu\pm3\sigma\to99.73\%$. In measurements the **uncertainty interval** conventionally uses $P=95\%$, i.e. $\pm1.96\sigma$ (often approximated as $\pm2\sigma$).

blocks.title: Worked: a Normal probability via Z

blocks.text: 4. Central Limit Theorem and the standard error

blocks.title: Central Limit Theorem (consequence for the mean)
blocks.caption: The superposition (sum) of many independent random variables is **Normal**, with **no restriction** on the distribution of the $X_i$; exact for $n\to\infty$, a good approximation for $n\ge30$. So the sample mean is normally distributed with the same centre $\mu$ but a **narrower** spread.

blocks.text: The spread of the **sample mean** is $\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}[\bar X_n]}=\sigma/\sqrt n$, the **standard error**. The population scatters with $\sigma$, but the *average* of $n$ values scatters only with $\sigma/\sqrt n$: taking more data makes the estimate of $\mu$ more precise — but only as $\sqrt n$ (to halve the standard error you need 4× the data).
5. Confidence intervals

A point estimate is not enough; we attach an **interval** that contains the true parameter with a stated **confidence level** $1-\alpha$:
$$\mathbb{P}[L_L\le\theta\le L_U]=1-\alpha,$$
where $\alpha$ is the **risk of error of the first kind** (conventionally $\alpha=5\%$, so $P=95\%$ in measurements).

blocks.title: Confidence interval for μ
blocks.tex: \text{known }\sigma:\ \bar X_n\pm z_{1-\alpha/2}\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}\qquad\quad \text{unknown }\sigma:\ \bar X_n\pm t_{n-1,\,1-\alpha/2}\frac{s}{\sqrt n}
blocks.caption: With the population $\sigma$ **known** use the standard normal $z$ (e.g. $z_{0.975}=1.96$ for 95%). With $\sigma$ **unknown** and estimated by $s$ use the **Student $t$** with $n-1$ degrees of freedom — wider, to account for the extra uncertainty of estimating $\sigma$.

blocks.title: What a confidence interval really means
blocks.text: It is **wrong** to say 'in 95% of cases $\mu$ falls inside *this* interval' ($\mu$ is fixed, the interval is random). The **correct** statement: over all possible samples of $n$, the set of intervals $\bar X_n\pm z_{1-\alpha/2}\,\sigma/\sqrt n$ would contain the unknown $\mu$ in $(1-\alpha)$ of cases.

question.prompt: 200 gearboxes (population normal, $\sigma=3250$ km) have sample mean life $\bar x=44500$ km. The half-width of the 95% confidence interval for the mean ($z_{0.975}=1.96$) is closest to:
question.explanation: Half-width $=z_{0.975}\,\sigma/\sqrt n=1.96\times3250/\sqrt{200}=1.96\times229.8\approx450$ km (so the interval is about $44050$–$44950$ km). **A** forgets to divide by $\sqrt n$ ($1.96\times3250\approx6370$). **C** uses $\sigma$ itself. **D** divides by $n$ instead of $\sqrt n$. The standard error is $\sigma/\sqrt n$, not $\sigma$ or $\sigma/n$.
question.theory: CI for $\mu$ (known $\sigma$): $\bar x\pm z_{1-\alpha/2}\,\sigma/\sqrt n$; the $\sqrt n$ comes from the standard error of the mean.
question.source: Lesson 5 part 2 (interval estimation of the expected value)

blocks.text: 6. The three sampling distributions
Three continuous distributions arise when we work with **samples** of a normal population, each answering a different inference question.

blocks.term: Student t — the mean with unknown σ
blocks.text: The **Student** distribution is bell-shaped like the Normal but, for **few data**, has heavier tails; its shape depends on the **degrees of freedom** $\nu=n-1$. As $n$ grows it becomes **asymptotic to the Normal**. Use it to build the **confidence interval of the mean when $\sigma$ is unknown** (estimated by $s$): $\bar X_n\pm t_{n-1,1-\alpha/2}\,s/\sqrt n$. Example: $\nu=4$ (i.e. $n=5$) at 95% gives $t=2.78$, noticeably larger than the normal $1.96$.

blocks.title: Student t distribution explorer
blocks.caption: Lower the degrees of freedom $\nu$ to fatten the tails (more uncertainty with few data); raise $\nu$ and watch the curve converge to the standard Normal.

blocks.term: Pearson χ² — the variance of a normal population
blocks.text:
Pearson introduced the **$\chi^2$** variable to describe the distribution of the variance estimator $s_n^2$ of a normal population. Since $(n-1)\,s^2/\sigma^2$ follows a $\chi^2$ with $n-1$ degrees of freedom, the **confidence interval for $\sigma^2$** is
$$(n-1)\frac{s^2}{\chi^2_2}<\sigma^2<(n-1)\frac{s^2}{\chi^2_1}.$$
It quantifies how much **random variability** affects the estimate of the variance — and shows why you need **many data** to pin $\sigma$ down.

blocks.title: Chi-square distribution explorer
blocks.caption: Change the degrees of freedom and see the right-skewed shape; it controls the confidence interval for the variance $\sigma^2$ of a normal population.

blocks.term: Fisher F — the ratio of two variances
blocks.text: R. A. Fisher introduced **$F$** for the distribution of the **ratio of two sample variances**: $F_{n_1-1,\,n_2-1}=\dfrac{s_1^2/\sigma_1^2}{s_2^2/\sigma_2^2}$, with the numerator evaluated on $n_1-1$ and the denominator on $n_2-1$ degrees of freedom. Use it to build the **confidence interval of a variance ratio** and to **compare the variances of two samples** (its $F$ values depend on *two* degrees-of-freedom and the probability, so they need a whole table, not one column).

blocks.title: Fisher F distribution explorer
blocks.caption: Set the two degrees of freedom $(m,n)$ and watch the shape change; the F distribution governs comparisons of two variances (the variance ratio).

blocks.title: Which sampling distribution for which job?
blocks.text:
- **Student t** → confidence interval / test of the **mean** when $\sigma$ is **unknown** ($\nu=n-1$).
- **Pearson $\chi^2$** → confidence interval / test of the **variance** $\sigma^2$ of one normal population ($\nu=n-1$).
- **Fisher F** → compare **two variances** (their ratio), with $(n_1-1,\,n_2-1)$ degrees of freedom.
When $\sigma$ is **known**, the mean uses the standard normal $z$, not $t$.

practice.prompt: Why is the sample variance computed with a denominator of $n-1$ rather than $n$?

options.content: Because one degree of freedom is used estimating the mean, so $n-1$ makes $s_n^2$ an unbiased (correct) estimator of $\sigma^2$.

practice.explanation: The deviations are taken from the sample mean, which is computed from the data; only $n-1$ deviations are free, so dividing by $n-1$ removes the bias and makes $\mathbb{E}[s_n^2]=\sigma^2$. **A** speed is irrelevant. **C** is a false ordering. **D** the correction is needed for any sample, not because the population is infinite.
practice.theory: Degrees of freedom: estimating $\mu$ from the same data costs one df, so $s_n^2=\tfrac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i-\bar X_n)^2$ is the unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$ (Excel VAR.S).

practice.prompt: The sample mean $\bar X_n$ is described as a correct and efficient estimator of $\mu$ because:

options.content: $\mathbb{E}[\bar X_n]=\mu$ (unbiased) and its variance $\sigma^2/n\to 0$ (consistent, minimal spread)
it is always exactly equal to $\mu$
it ignores the variance of the data
it requires the population to be normal

practice.explanation: Unbiasedness ($\mathbb{E}[\bar X_n]=\mu$) makes it **correct**; the shrinking variance $\sigma^2/n$ makes it **consistent** and, among unbiased estimators, **efficient**. **B** is false — an estimate varies from sample to sample. **C** is irrelevant. **D** the unbiasedness holds for any distribution with mean $\mu$, normality is not required.
practice.theory: Correct: $\mathbb{E}[T]=\theta$. Consistent: $\mathrm{Var}[T]\to0$. Efficient: smallest variance among correct estimators.

practice.prompt: For a Normal distribution, the interval $\mu\pm1.96\sigma$ contains approximately what fraction of the values?
practice.explanation: $\mu\pm1.96\sigma$ captures the central **95%** (the conventional measurement uncertainty level). **A** is $\mu\pm1\sigma$ (68.3%). **B** is $\mu\pm1.645\sigma$ (90%). **D** is $\mu\pm3\sigma$ (99.73%). The familiar $\pm2\sigma$ gives 95.45%, close to but not exactly the 95% of $\pm1.96\sigma$.

practice.prompt: $X\sim N(\mu=50,\sigma=4)$. The standardised value $Z$ corresponding to $x=58$ is:
practice.explanation: $Z=(x-\mu)/\sigma=(58-50)/4=8/4=2.0$. **B** is the numerator $x-\mu$ without dividing by $\sigma$. **C** inverts the ratio. **D** is meaningless. Standardising converts any normal value to the $N(0,1)$ scale so tables/areas apply.
practice.theory: Standardisation: $Z=(X-\mu)/\sigma\sim N(0,1)$; then probabilities come from $F(z)$.

practice.prompt: The Central Limit Theorem implies that the sample mean of $n$ i.i.d. values with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$ is approximately:
practice.explanation: By the CLT consequence, $\bar X_n\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2/n)$: same centre, variance divided by $n$. **A** is the population, not the mean. **C** describes the *sum* $\sum X_i\sim N(n\mu,n\sigma^2)$, not the average. **D** the limiting shape is normal, not uniform — regardless of the original distribution.
practice.theory: CLT: $\bar X_n\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2/n)$ for $n\ge30$, with standard error $\sigma/\sqrt n$; no restriction on the parent distribution.

practice.prompt: A process has $\sigma=12$. To estimate $\mu$, you take $n=36$ measurements. The standard error of the sample mean is:
practice.explanation: Standard error $=\sigma/\sqrt n=12/\sqrt{36}=12/6=2$. **A** uses $\sigma$ itself (the population spread, not the mean's). **C** divides by $n=36$ instead of $\sqrt n$. **D** multiplies. The mean of 36 values scatters 6× less than a single value.
practice.theory: Standard error of the mean = $\sigma/\sqrt n$; precision improves only as $\sqrt n$.

practice.prompt: You build a confidence interval for the mean of a normal population, but $\sigma$ is **unknown** and estimated from $n=5$ data. Which value scales the half-width $s/\sqrt n$ at 95%?
practice.explanation: With $\sigma$ unknown and estimated by $s$, the mean's interval uses the **Student $t$** with $\nu=n-1=4$ df: $t_{4,0.975}=2.78$, larger than the normal $1.96$ to account for estimating $\sigma$. **A** $z$ applies only when $\sigma$ is *known*. **B** $\chi^2$ is for the variance interval. **C** $F$ compares two variances. Few data ⇒ heavier $t$ tails ⇒ wider interval.
practice.theory: Mean, $\sigma$ known → $z$; mean, $\sigma$ unknown → Student $t$ ($\nu=n-1$); variance → $\chi^2$; two variances → $F$.
practice.source: Lesson 5 part 2 (interval estimation with unknown variance; Student distribution)

practice.prompt: Which distribution would you use to build a confidence interval for the **variance** $\sigma^2$ of a single normally-distributed population?
practice.explanation: $(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2$ follows a **$\chi^2$** with $n-1$ df, which gives the confidence interval for $\sigma^2$. **A** Student $t$ is for the *mean* with unknown $\sigma$. **B** $Z$ is for the mean with known $\sigma$. **C** Fisher $F$ compares *two* variances. The $\chi^2$ describes the distribution of the variance estimator of a normal population.
practice.theory: $\chi^2$ ↔ variance of one normal population; CI: $(n-1)s^2/\chi^2_2<\sigma^2<(n-1)s^2/\chi^2_1$.

practice.prompt: Two independent samples have sample variances $s_1^2$ and $s_2^2$. To test/compare their two variances (the variance ratio), you use:

options.content: the Fisher F distribution with $(n_1-1,\,n_2-1)$ degrees of freedom

practice.explanation: The ratio of two sample variances follows the **Fisher F** distribution with degrees of freedom $(n_1-1,\,n_2-1)$. **A** Student $t$ handles a mean with unknown $\sigma$. **B** Poisson is a discrete distribution for rare counts. **D** the normal is for a mean with known $\sigma$. $F$ needs *two* df because two variances are involved.
practice.theory: Fisher $F_{n_1-1,n_2-1}=\dfrac{s_1^2/\sigma_1^2}{s_2^2/\sigma_2^2}$ ↔ comparison of two variances (variance ratio).

practice.prompt: A normal population is sampled with $n=5$ ($\nu=4$), giving $s=3$. Using $\chi^2_{1}=0.48$ (2.5%) and $\chi^2_{2}=11.14$ (97.5%), the 95% confidence interval for $\sigma^2$ is closest to:
practice.explanation: $\sigma^2$ lies in $\left((n-1)\frac{s^2}{\chi^2_2},\ (n-1)\frac{s^2}{\chi^2_1}\right)=\left(4\cdot\frac{9}{11.14},\ 4\cdot\frac{9}{0.48}\right)=(3.2,\ 75)$. **B** just quotes the $\chi^2$ values. **C** is the interval for $\sigma$ ($1.8<\sigma<8.7$), not $\sigma^2$. **D** ignores the spread entirely. The very wide interval shows how poorly few data constrain the variance.
practice.theory: CI for variance: $(n-1)s^2/\chi^2_2<\sigma^2<(n-1)s^2/\chi^2_1$; with few df the interval is very wide.
